standards.title: Classification and Biodiversity
standards.description: Investigate the role of classification in ordering and organising the diversity of life on Earth and use and develop classification tools including dichotomous keys
standards.prior_knowledge: Students can name and describe familiar living things and sort them into broad groups (animals, plants). They have not studied formal taxonomy or constructed classification tools.
standards.y_goal: Students explain why classification systems are used, apply and construct dichotomous keys, and evaluate how classification tools order and organise biodiversity.

nodes.label: Purpose of classification
nodes.xmin: State at least two reasons why scientists classify living things; give one example of classification in everyday life
nodes.width_core: Compare two different classification systems; explain which is more useful for a given purpose and why
width_enrich_options: Investigate how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples classify living things on Country and compare with Linnaean taxonomy — what does each system prioritise?
Research how classification systems have changed over time through improvements in microscopy; explain what this reveals about scientific knowledge
Consider how classification supports identification and communication in a real scientific or practical context (e.g. biosecurity, medicine)
nodes.y_description: Students can explain why classification is useful for organising and communicating about living things

nodes.label: Taxonomy: major groups and kingdoms
nodes.xmin: Name the major kingdoms; correctly place 5 familiar organisms into their kingdom with justification using observable features
nodes.width_core: Sort a set of 12 organisms into kingdoms; identify one organism that is difficult to classify and explain why
width_enrich_options: Name and classify species using Linnaean hierarchy (kingdom → species); explain what each level represents
Research a boundary case (e.g. Euglena, virus) — does it fit the kingdom model? What does this reveal about classification systems?
Observe and identify similarities and differences of particular features within and between groups of organisms using provided specimens or images
nodes.y_description: Students can place organisms into major taxonomic groups using observable features and justify their classification

nodes.label: Using dichotomous keys
nodes.xmin: Use a provided dichotomous key to correctly identify at least 5 organisms; explain how each decision point works
nodes.width_core: Use two different keys for the same organism set; identify where they agree and disagree and explain why
width_enrich_options: Use a provided dichotomous key to identify organisms surveyed on a field trip or from local environment
Evaluate a flawed dichotomous key — identify the error, explain its consequence, and propose a correction
Compare a scientific dichotomous key with a First Nations classification system for the same organisms — what features does each prioritise?
nodes.hinge_reason: Using a key requires students to apply binary logic to observable features — a prerequisite for constructing keys. Misconceptions about feature selection cluster here and propagate directly into Node 4.
nodes.y_description: Students can use a dichotomous key to identify organisms by applying binary decisions based on observable features

nodes.label: Constructing dichotomous keys
nodes.xmin: Construct a simple dichotomous key for a set of 6 organisms using observable features; test it with a peer
nodes.width_core: Construct and refine a key for 10 organisms; document one revision made after peer testing and explain the improvement
width_enrich_options: Create and modify a dichotomous key to classify organisms into groups and subgroups; evaluate its effectiveness
Design a key for a novel organism set (e.g. everyday objects or invented creatures); reflect on what makes a feature scientifically useful
Construct a key based on First Nations classification criteria for local organisms and compare its structure with a Linnaean key
nodes.hinge_reason: Construction requires students to reverse-engineer the logic of classification — the deepest understanding of the standard. Assessment items directly test this. Errors here are not recoverable without significant reteaching.
nodes.y_description: Students can construct a dichotomous key that correctly classifies a set of organisms using observable features

nodes.label: Classification systems change over time
nodes.xmin: Give one example of a reclassification event; explain what new evidence caused the change
nodes.width_core: Trace the reclassification of one organism — what evidence existed before, what changed, and how did the scientific community respond?
width_enrich_options: Examine how biological classification has changed through improvements in microscopy and DNA analysis
Evaluate: is reclassification a sign that science is unreliable, or a sign that it works? Construct an evidence-based argument
Investigate how First Nations classification knowledge has contributed to or challenged scientific classification in specific cases
nodes.y_description: Students can explain how classification systems change in response to new evidence and evaluate what this reveals about scientific knowledge

standards.title: Food Webs and Ecosystems
standards.description: Use models, including food webs, to represent matter and energy flow in ecosystems and predict the impact of changing abiotic and biotic factors on populations
standards.prior_knowledge: Students can identify producers, consumers and decomposers and construct simple food chains. They have not studied energy flow quantitatively or modelled multi-factor ecosystem changes.
standards.y_goal: Students use food web models to represent matter and energy flow, and predict and explain population impacts of changes to abiotic and biotic factors.

nodes.label: From food chains to food webs
nodes.xmin: Construct a food web from a given organism list; identify producers, primary and secondary consumers, and decomposers including microorganisms
nodes.width_core: Compare a food chain and food web for the same ecosystem — explain what information is lost in the simpler model
width_enrich_options: Analyse food webs to show feeding relationships including the role of microorganisms in decomposition
Critique a published food web diagram — what is it missing, what assumptions does it make?
Investigate First Nations Australians' food webs in a specific region and compare with a scientific representation of the same ecosystem
nodes.y_description: Students can construct a food web and identify the roles of producers, consumers and decomposers including microorganisms

nodes.label: Matter and energy flow
nodes.xmin: Explain the direction of energy flow in a food web; state that energy is lost at each trophic level; distinguish energy flow from matter cycling
nodes.width_core: Use a given energy pyramid to explain why top predators are rare; connect to food web structure
width_enrich_options: Model how energy flows into and out of an ecosystem via food web pathways; represent quantitatively using the 10% rule
Compare energy flow in two different ecosystems (e.g. rainforest vs desert); explain structural differences
Analyse a real dataset of biomass at each trophic level; explain the pattern using energy flow principles
nodes.hinge_reason: Energy flow direction and loss is the conceptual engine behind all population prediction tasks. Students who conflate matter cycling with energy flow make systematic errors in Nodes 3 and 4. Primary misconception gateway for this standard.
nodes.y_description: Students can explain the direction and loss of energy through trophic levels and distinguish energy flow from matter cycling

nodes.label: Abiotic factors and population effects
nodes.xmin: Define abiotic factor; give three examples; explain how one abiotic change would affect one population in a given food web
nodes.width_core: Trace the cascade effect of one abiotic change (e.g. drought, temperature rise) through at least 3 populations with reasoning at each step
width_enrich_options: Examine how seasonal changes or habitat destruction impact abiotic factors and cause population changes
Compare cascade effects of two different abiotic changes on the same ecosystem — which is more destabilising and why?
Investigate First Nations Australians' fire management practices and their effect on abiotic factors and flora/fauna distribution
nodes.y_description: Students can predict and explain how a change to an abiotic factor cascades through populations in a food web

nodes.label: Biotic factors and population effects
nodes.xmin: Define biotic factor; give three examples; predict the effect of removing one species from a given food web with reasoning
nodes.width_core: Analyse a trophic cascade case study (e.g. wolf reintroduction in Yellowstone); explain the chain of biotic effects with evidence
width_enrich_options: Predict effects on local ecosystems when pollinators or predators are removed or introduced
Investigate First Nations Australians' responses to invasive species and their effect on food webs communities depend on
Evaluate an invasive species scenario — construct food webs before and after, predict population effects, and propose a management response
nodes.hinge_reason: Predicting biotic cascade effects requires holding the whole food web in mind simultaneously — the deepest application of the model. Assessment tasks typically test this directly. Students who haven't secured energy flow (Node 2) make compounding errors here.
nodes.y_description: Students can predict and explain how a change to a biotic factor cascades through populations in a food web

nodes.label: Models, limitations and predictions
nodes.xmin: Identify one limitation of food web models; explain how this affects the reliability of predictions made from them
nodes.width_core: Compare a simple and complex model of the same ecosystem — explain what each can and cannot predict
width_enrich_options: Evaluate a real scientific food web model used in a conservation decision — what did it predict, what did it miss?
Consider how introduction of a species impacts abiotic and biotic factors beyond immediate food web effects
Research how First Nations ecological knowledge has improved or challenged scientific food web models in a specific case
nodes.y_description: Students can evaluate the limitations of food web models and explain how these affect the reliability of predictions

standards.title: Earth-Sun-Moon System
standards.description: Model cyclic changes in the relative positions of the Earth, sun and moon and explain how these cycles cause eclipses and influence predictable phenomena on Earth, including seasons and tides
standards.prior_knowledge: Students can describe day/night as caused by Earth's rotation and identify moon phases from observation. They have not modelled 3D spatial relationships or explained tides and eclipses mechanistically.
standards.y_goal: Students model and explain how cyclic changes in Earth-sun-moon positions cause eclipses, seasons and tides.

nodes.label: Earth's rotation, revolution and axial tilt
nodes.xmin: Distinguish rotation from revolution; link rotation to day/night and revolution to the year; identify that Earth's axis is tilted
nodes.width_core: Use a physical or digital model to demonstrate both cycles simultaneously; explain what an observer on Earth would experience across a year
width_enrich_options: Use physical models or virtual simulations to explain how Earth's tilt and position relative to the sun causes differences in light intensity
Investigate First Nations Australians' calendars and how they are used to predict seasonal changes
Explain why the length of daylight changes across a year at different latitudes using a model
nodes.y_description: Students can distinguish Earth's rotation from revolution and explain how axial tilt relates to observable changes across a year

nodes.label: Moon phases and the lunar cycle
nodes.xmin: Correctly sequence all 8 moon phases; explain that phases result from the moon's position relative to Earth and sun, not Earth's shadow
nodes.width_core: Model moon phases using a light source and spheres; predict the phase visible from Earth at a given moon position
width_enrich_options: Analyse a month of real moon phase data — identify the pattern, calculate the period, identify anomalies
Research knowledges held by First Nations Australians regarding the phases of the moon and the lunar cycle
Investigate how lunar calendars from different cultures use moon phases differently and why
nodes.y_description: Students can explain moon phases as a result of the moon's position relative to Earth and sun and sequence them correctly

nodes.label: Eclipses: solar and lunar
nodes.xmin: Explain the conditions required for a solar and lunar eclipse; correctly distinguish umbra from penumbra; explain why eclipses don't occur every month
nodes.width_core: Model both eclipse types using a light source; explain the role of the moon's orbital tilt in eclipse frequency
width_enrich_options: Use physical models or virtual simulations to explain cyclic patterns of lunar and solar eclipses
Research First Nations Australians' oral traditions and cultural recordings of solar and lunar eclipses; compare with contemporary understandings
Analyse historical eclipse records — explain why ancient cultures found eclipses unpredictable and what understanding was missing
nodes.hinge_reason: Eclipse explanation requires 3D spatial reasoning about three bodies simultaneously — the hardest conceptual step in this standard. Students who rely on 2D diagrams alone consistently misexplain why eclipses are rare. Directly affects assessment performance.
nodes.y_description: Students can explain the conditions required for solar and lunar eclipses and why eclipses do not occur every month

nodes.label: Seasons
nodes.xmin: Explain that seasons are caused by Earth's axial tilt, not distance from the sun; correctly link tilt to solar angle and day length
nodes.width_core: Use a model to demonstrate why the Southern and Northern hemispheres experience opposite seasons simultaneously
width_enrich_options: Compare seasonal patterns at the equator, temperate zones, and poles — explain differences using the tilt model
Investigate First Nations Australians' seasonal calendars (e.g. six-season calendars) and compare with the four-season European model
Analyse real insolation data for two locations at different latitudes across a year; explain the patterns using the tilt model
nodes.hinge_reason: The distance misconception (seasons caused by proximity to sun) is extremely persistent and directly tested in assessments. Without resolving it here, students produce confidently wrong answers. High width investment has strong downstream payoff.
nodes.y_description: Students can explain seasons as caused by axial tilt and correctly relate tilt to solar angle and day length at different latitudes

nodes.label: Tides
nodes.xmin: Explain that tides are caused by the moon's gravitational pull; identify spring and neap tides and the moon positions that cause them
nodes.width_core: Analyse a tide chart for a coastal location — identify spring and neap cycles and connect them to the lunar calendar
width_enrich_options: Examine the gravitational attraction of the moon and sun on Earth's oceans; explain how relative positions result in tidal variations
Research how First Nations Australians used tidal patterns and connection between lunar cycle and ocean tides for resource management
Investigate how tidal patterns differ between locations and explain using the moon-sun-Earth model
nodes.y_description: Students can explain tides as caused by the moon's gravitational pull and distinguish spring from neap tides by moon position

standards.title: Forces and Motion
standards.description: Investigate and represent balanced and unbalanced forces, including gravitational force, acting on objects, and relate changes in an object's motion to its mass and the magnitude and direction of forces acting on it
standards.prior_knowledge: Students can identify pushes and pulls as forces and know that gravity pulls objects toward Earth. They have not represented forces as vectors, analysed balanced/unbalanced states, or applied F=ma reasoning.
standards.y_goal: Students represent and explain how balanced and unbalanced forces, including gravity, relate to changes in an object's motion in terms of mass, magnitude and direction.

nodes.label: Forces as vectors: magnitude and direction
nodes.xmin: Represent at least two forces on an object using arrows with correct relative size and direction; name the forces
nodes.width_core: Draw force diagrams for 4 different scenarios (stationary, accelerating, decelerating, falling); justify arrow choices
width_enrich_options: Measure the magnitude of forces using a force meter; represent magnitude and direction using force arrow diagrams
Analyse a real photograph of an object in motion — identify all forces acting and represent them in a diagram
Investigate forces acting on boomerangs and how First Peoples of Australia designed an aerofoil profile — represent forces involved
nodes.y_description: Students can represent forces on an object as vectors showing correct magnitude and direction and name the forces

nodes.label: Balanced forces and equilibrium
nodes.xmin: Define balanced forces; correctly identify whether forces are balanced in a given diagram; state that balanced forces mean no change in motion
nodes.width_core: Analyse 6 scenarios — classify each as balanced or unbalanced; predict the motion state for each with reasoning
width_enrich_options: Analyse the effect of balanced and unbalanced forces on an object's motion including starting, stopping and changing direction
Investigate a real engineering structure (e.g. bridge, building) — identify how balanced forces are maintained
Investigate how simple machines such as levers and pulleys change the magnitude of force needed — represent forces involved
nodes.hinge_reason: The misconception that balanced forces mean no forces (rather than equal and opposite) is extremely common and directly undermines Newton's First Law. This error propagates into every subsequent node.
nodes.y_description: Students can identify whether forces on an object are balanced or unbalanced and predict the resulting motion state

nodes.label: Unbalanced forces and changes in motion
nodes.xmin: Explain that unbalanced forces cause a change in motion (speed or direction); give two examples with force diagrams
nodes.width_core: Analyse motion scenarios — identify the unbalanced force, predict the change in motion, draw the force diagram
width_enrich_options: Investigate the effects of applying different forces to familiar objects of the same and different mass
Design a fair test to investigate how the magnitude of an unbalanced force affects the rate of motion change
Investigate forces through application of traditional tools such as spearthrowers used by First Peoples of Australia — analyse the forces involved
nodes.y_description: Students can explain that unbalanced forces cause a change in motion and represent this with force diagrams

nodes.label: Gravitational force and mass
nodes.xmin: Distinguish mass from weight; explain gravitational force as dependent on mass; calculate weight given mass and gravitational field strength
nodes.width_core: Compare gravitational force on the same object on Earth, the Moon, and Mars — explain differences using gravitational field strength
width_enrich_options: Investigate how Earth's gravitational force pulls objects to the centre of Earth and how its magnitude relates to mass
Examine how gravity affects objects in space including moons, planets, stars and galaxies
Evaluate a common misconception (e.g. 'objects fall at different rates based on mass') using experimental evidence; construct a refutation
nodes.hinge_reason: Mass/weight confusion is the most persistent misconception in this standard and is directly assessed. Students who conflate the two make systematic errors in force calculations and cannot correctly explain gravitational scenarios.
nodes.y_description: Students can distinguish mass from weight, explain gravitational force as dependent on mass, and calculate weight from mass

nodes.label: Relating force, mass and motion change
nodes.xmin: State the relationship between force, mass and acceleration qualitatively; predict whether a heavier or lighter object will accelerate more under the same force
nodes.width_core: Analyse data from a force investigation — identify the pattern between force, mass and acceleration; represent graphically
width_enrich_options: Evaluate a real-world application (e.g. vehicle safety, rocket launch) — explain how engineers manipulate force and mass
Investigate how simple machines change force magnitude for a task; analyse the trade-offs involved
Analyse the forces acting on a boomerang in flight — explain how design variables affect the motion outcome
nodes.y_description: Students can describe the qualitative relationship between force, mass and acceleration and interpret data from force investigations

standards.title: Particle Theory and Properties
standards.description: Use particle theory to describe the arrangement of particles in a substance, including the motion of and attraction between particles, and relate this to the properties of the substance
standards.prior_knowledge: Students know that matter exists as solids, liquids and gases and can describe observable properties. They have not used particle theory to explain properties or modelled particle behaviour at a submicroscopic level.
standards.y_goal: Students use particle theory to explain and predict the physical properties of substances in terms of particle arrangement, motion and attraction.

nodes.label: Introducing the particle model
nodes.xmin: State the key ideas of particle theory (particles, spacing, motion, attraction); correctly match particle diagrams to solid, liquid and gas
nodes.width_core: Explain three observable properties of a substance using particle theory language; draw and annotate particle diagrams
width_enrich_options: Use and construct models, diagrams or virtual simulations to represent particle arrangement in different states
Evaluate the particle model as a model — what does it explain well, what does it not explain, and what evidence supports it?
Compare the particle model with how materials properties are described in Design and Technologies contexts
nodes.y_description: Students can match particle diagrams to states of matter and state the key ideas of particle theory

nodes.label: Particle arrangement, motion and attraction in each state
nodes.xmin: Describe particle arrangement, motion and attraction for each state; explain why solids hold shape and gases fill containers
nodes.width_core: Compare particle diagrams for 6 substances — rank by particle spacing and predict relative density; check against data
width_enrich_options: Relate motion and energy of particles to distances between particles in different states of the same substance
Compare attractive forces in solid, liquid and gaseous states and relate to relative position and movement of particles
Investigate an anomalous case (e.g. ice floating on water) — explain using particle theory and evaluate whether the basic model needs refinement
nodes.hinge_reason: Particle arrangement is the foundation of all property explanations. Students who treat particle diagrams as decorative rather than explanatory cannot use the model to reason. Primary misconception gateway — errors propagate into every subsequent node and are directly assessed.
nodes.y_description: Students can describe particle arrangement, motion and attraction for each state and explain observable properties using this model

nodes.label: Explaining physical properties using particle theory
nodes.xmin: Use particle theory to explain at least two physical properties (e.g. compressibility, viscosity, density) of a given substance
nodes.width_core: Compare the physical properties of three substances using particle theory; explain why properties differ in terms of arrangement, motion and attraction
width_enrich_options: Investigate properties of materials such as density, melting point and compressibility and explain in terms of particle arrangement
Explain the process of diffusion in a liquid and a gas in terms of particle motion
Predict the properties of an unfamiliar substance given only its particle diagram; justify predictions and test against published data
nodes.hinge_reason: This is the key application node — using the model to explain rather than just describe. Assessment tasks require explanatory use of particle theory. Students who can only recall diagrams without explanation cannot access higher marks.
nodes.y_description: Students can use particle theory to explain physical properties of substances including compressibility, viscosity and density

nodes.label: Changes of state and energy
nodes.xmin: Explain melting, freezing, evaporation and condensation using particle theory; correctly identify energy input/output for each change
nodes.width_core: Analyse a heating/cooling curve — identify each state change, explain plateau regions using particle theory, connect to energy changes
width_enrich_options: Examine how changing motion and energy of particles is affected by heat energy absorbed or released
Model changes in particle arrangement as substances change state using diagrams or virtual simulations
Investigate sublimation or a non-standard state change — explain using particle theory and evaluate whether the basic model is sufficient
nodes.y_description: Students can explain changes of state using particle theory and correctly identify energy input or output for each change

nodes.label: Particle theory as a model: scope and limits
nodes.xmin: Identify one phenomenon particle theory explains well and one it does not explain; give a reason for each
nodes.width_core: Compare particle theory with an alternative model — explain what each is designed to explain and why scientists use multiple models
width_enrich_options: Research a historical development of particle theory — how did evidence change the model over time?
Analyse how characteristics and properties of materials are used in Design and Technologies to create solutions — connect to particle-level explanations
Evaluate published claims about a material's properties using particle theory as a critical lens
nodes.y_description: Students can identify the scope and limitations of the particle model and explain what evidence supports it

standards.title: Pure Substances and Mixtures
standards.description: Use a particle model to describe differences between pure substances and mixtures and apply understanding of properties of substances to separate mixtures
standards.prior_knowledge: Students can classify materials as solids, liquids or gases and have an introductory particle model from AC9S7U05. They have not formally distinguished pure substances from mixtures at the particle level or applied separation techniques systematically.
standards.y_goal: Students use a particle model to explain the difference between pure substances and mixtures, and select and apply appropriate separation techniques based on properties of substances.

nodes.label: Pure substances vs mixtures at the particle level
nodes.xmin: Distinguish pure substance from mixture using particle diagrams; classify 5 familiar materials correctly with justification
nodes.width_core: Draw particle diagrams for a pure substance, a homogeneous mixture and a heterogeneous mixture; explain the key differences
width_enrich_options: Use representations of particles to show the difference between samples of pure substances and mixtures; identify examples of each in everyday contexts
Examine different solutions and identify the solvent and solute; represent at the particle level
Investigate a borderline case (e.g. alloy, saltwater at different concentrations) — is it pure or a mixture? What does this reveal about the definition?
nodes.hinge_reason: The pure/mixture distinction at the particle level is prerequisite for all separation technique selection. Students who cannot make this distinction select inappropriate techniques and cannot explain their choices — the primary assessment task.
nodes.y_description: Students can distinguish pure substances from mixtures using particle diagrams and classify familiar materials correctly

nodes.label: Properties as the basis for separation
nodes.xmin: State that separation techniques exploit differences in physical properties; match at least three property differences to a separation technique
nodes.width_core: Analyse a mixture — identify relevant property differences and justify which technique is most appropriate, including why others would not work
width_enrich_options: Analyse how physical properties of substances in mixtures (particle size, density, volatility) determine the separation technique used
Explore and compare separation methods used in a variety of situations such as in the home, recycling industries and water purification
Evaluate a separation scenario with multiple possible techniques — rank by efficiency, cost and practicality
nodes.hinge_reason: Understanding that techniques exploit property differences (not just 'removing things') is the conceptual key. Without this, students apply techniques by rote and cannot explain or adapt when scenarios change. Directly assessed.
nodes.y_description: Students can explain that separation techniques exploit differences in physical properties and match property differences to techniques

nodes.label: Filtration and evaporation
nodes.xmin: Correctly perform or describe filtration and evaporation; identify a mixture each technique is appropriate for and explain why using property differences
nodes.width_core: Design a procedure to separate a given mixture using filtration and/or evaporation; predict and explain the result at the particle level
width_enrich_options: Investigate and use filtration and evaporation in a range of practical contexts; evaluate effectiveness
Investigate separation techniques used by First Nations Australians such as hand-picking, sieving, winnowing and filtering — explain the property differences each exploits
Investigate the effect of a variable (e.g. filter paper grade, temperature) on separation efficiency; analyse results using particle theory
nodes.y_description: Students can perform or describe filtration and evaporation and justify their use for appropriate mixtures

nodes.label: Distillation and chromatography
nodes.xmin: Describe the principle of distillation and chromatography; identify a mixture each is appropriate for and explain why
nodes.width_core: Compare distillation and evaporation — explain when each is appropriate and why, using particle theory and property differences
width_enrich_options: Investigate crystallisation, chromatography and distillation; explain the property differences each exploits
Investigate separation techniques used by First Nations Australians such as yandying, cold-pressing and steam distilling — connect to property principles
Analyse a chromatography result — calculate Rf values, identify components, evaluate what the result reveals about mixture composition
nodes.y_description: Students can describe the principles of distillation and chromatography and identify mixtures each technique is appropriate for

nodes.label: Selecting and justifying separation techniques
nodes.xmin: Given an unfamiliar mixture, select an appropriate technique and justify the choice using property differences
nodes.width_core: Design a multi-step separation procedure for a mixture with three components; justify each step and predict the outcome
width_enrich_options: Evaluate a real-world separation process (e.g. water purification, mining, food processing) — identify techniques used and property differences exploited
Compare industrial and First Nations separation techniques for the same type of mixture — analyse efficiency, sustainability and cultural significance
Design and evaluate an original separation procedure for a novel mixture; assess against criteria of efficiency, cost and practicality
nodes.y_description: Students can select and justify an appropriate separation technique for an unfamiliar mixture using property differences
